title: Mud on Carpet
stainChemistry: Combination stains contain protein, fat, and tannin or mineral components. Mud contains organic material (plant matter, tannins), clay/mineral particles, and sometimes protein components. Each component requires different chemistry: protein needs detergent, fat needs solvent, tannin needs peroxide, minerals need acid. Standard approach: let mud dry completely first (wet scrubbing drives particles deeper), then detergent, then solvent, then tannin treatment.
whyThisWorks: This combination stain contains multiple components — organic pigments, clay, and tannins — that each need separate treatment. Mud soaks through carpet into the padding below. Surface cleaning won't reach it — the stain hides underneath and wicks back up as the material dries. Dwell time matters more than scrubbing force.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply NSD and work gently into the stain. This NSD targets the protein component and begins emulsifying surface oils. Blot thoroughly between passes.
2. Apply POG or VDS to extract the oil and grease component of the stain. Blot with clean absorbent cloth. This targets what the NSD couldn't fully emulsify.
3. If browning or color remains, apply Tannin to target the tannin component. This addresses the plant-based pigments that give the stain its color. Tamp gently.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize all residual chemistry and restore the fabric. Blot dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — hot water can set protein component permanently
warnings: Applying all treatments simultaneously — creates a chemical slurry that's harder to remove
Using hot water early — can set the protein component permanently
Not rinsing completely between treatments — old chemistry interferes with new treatment
Over-agitating the stain — spreads each component deeper into the fiber

homeSolutions.agent: WAIT — let the mud dry fully before doing anything else
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the most important instruction for mud on carpet. Wet mud is clay particles suspended in water. If you scrub, blot, or wipe it while wet, you grind those particles DEEPER into the carpet fibers and the pad — and the stain becomes much harder to remove. Instead: block the area off, keep pets and kids out, and wait. Let it dry completely. Small spot: 1–3 hours. Large spot: 4–8 hours. Point a fan at it to speed drying. Do not use a hair dryer or heat.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden spot
homeSolutions.instruction: While the mud dries, find a hidden patch of the same carpet. Dab a drop of Dawn + cold water solution, blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the fiber holds its color, you're safe. If dye transfers, you'll be blotting with water only in the later steps and should go straight to a pro if the mud doesn't lift cleanly.

homeSolutions.agent: Break up + brush off the dry crust
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the mud is fully dry and crumbly, gently break up the surface crust with a soft brush or the edge of a credit card. You should see chunks release from the pile. Then VACUUM thoroughly — multiple slow passes from different directions to lift particles out of the base of the fibers. 60–80% of a mud stain comes up at this step if you let it dry first. If you see a brown residue still, that's the tannin + fine clay that needs chemistry.

Mix one tablespoon of clear Dawn dish soap with two cups of cold water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the area — outside edges in. Let it sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn lifts any oil or plant-matter residue the vacuum couldn't get. Repeat the soap-and-blot cycle once or twice.

If a brown shadow remains after the soap cycle, that's tannin from plant matter in the mud. Dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on the spot with a white cloth, let sit 5 minutes, blot with a damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes tannin pigment. Skip this on dark or patterned carpet — check the patch-test result from step 2 first.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. Test on your hidden patch first — carpet dye varies in bleach tolerance.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dab onto the treated area. Vinegar does two things here: (1) neutralizes any peroxide residue, and (2) acts as a mild mineral remover for lingering clay particles. Blot dry.

Stack six to ten sheets of white paper towels over the area, weigh them down with a heavy book, and leave overnight. This pulls any residual moisture (and the last of the mineral/tannin particles) up out of the pad before it can wick back as a ring. Don't vacuum or walk on the area until fully dry.

Once fully dry, vacuum the area again — slow, multiple directions — to pick up any loose particles the chemistry loosened. Inspect in daylight. Fresh mud should be fully gone. If a shadow remains and the carpet passes the patch test, repeat steps 4–6 once more.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the mud was clay-heavy (red clay, potting soil), covered a large area, or survived two home rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Mud on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, [fresh/aged], let dry fully and vacuumed, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar rinse. No hot water used." They have truck-mount hot-water extraction + tannin/mineral formulas that can finish what vacuum-plus-home-chemistry can't.

steps: 1. Let mud dry completely before brushing off — wet scrubbing drives particles deeper.
2. Once dry, brush or vacuum off the loose dry material.
3. Mix mild dish soap into cold water. Blot the solution onto the stain.
4. Let it sit for 5–10 minutes. Blot with a clean white cloth.
5. Blot with a cloth dampened with plain water to rinse. Place a stack of paper towels over the spot and weigh them down. Let it dry completely.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain is still visible after multiple treatments, it may be permanently set. Consider professional cleaning before attempting further home treatment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing damages carpet fibers and spreads the stain.
Place paper towels over the wet spot and weigh them down to prevent wicking overnight.
Let mud dry first — never scrub wet mud, it drives the stain deeper.

neverDo: Scrub wet mud — drives particles deeper into carpet fibers
Apply all treatments simultaneously — creates a harder-to-remove chemical slurry
Use hot water early — can set protein component permanently
fiberSensitivities: Combination stains on delicate fibers require careful chemistry sequencing. Protein fibers (silk, wool) need shorter dwell times and milder concentrations for each treatment.

escalation.when: Sequential treating fails, or stain components have cross-linked together
escalation.whatToTell: Mud stain on carpet, combination of organic pigment, clay, and tannins. Treated at home with NSD. Please use multi-step sequential protocol.

contextQuestions: Was the mud tracked in from outdoors (clay-heavy) or from a garden/potting soil?
Is the stain fresh or has it dried?
Is the carpet synthetic or wool/natural fiber?
Was there grass mixed with the mud?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Clay-heavy mud is more tenacious and may need an acid mineral rinse step. Garden/potting soil often has a higher organic content. Grass mixed with mud adds a tannin/chlorophyll component. Natural fiber carpets require gentler chemistry.
